AB-Alberta.summary: In Alberta, Canada, adult-use cannabis is legal and regulated under federal law through the Cannabis Act, with provincial oversight managed by Alberta Gaming, Liquor and Cannabis (AGLC). Retail cannabis is sold through private licensed stores and online via the AGLC’s official platform, and there ar

AK-Alaska.summary: Alaska has both a medical and adult-use cannabis program, with adult-use legalized through Ballot Measure 2 in 2014. The state allows licensed businesses to cultivate, manufacture, and sell cannabis products to adults 21 and over, regulated by the Alaska Alcohol and Marijuana Control Office (AMCO).

AL-Alabama.seProgramDetails: The commission shall ensure that at least one-fourth of all licenses, or in the case of Section 20-2A-67, one-fifth of all licenses, are awarded to business entities at least 51 percent of which are owned by members of a minority group or, in the case of a corporation, at least 51 percent of the shares of the corporation are owned by members of a minority group, and are managed and controlled by members of a minority group in its daily operations. For purposes of this subsection, minority group means individuals of African American, Native American, Asian, or Hispanic descent.
AL-Alabama.summary: Alabama has a medical cannabis program in place but does not allow adult-use cannabis. The Alabama Medical Cannabis Commission (AMCC) oversees the program, which permits licensed cultivation, processing, dispensing, and physician certification for qualifying patients with specific conditions. Sales

AR-Arkansas.summary: Arkansas operates a medical cannabis program established by voter approval in 2016 through the Arkansas Medical Marijuana Amendment (Amendment 98). The program allows registered patients with qualifying conditions to purchase cannabis from licensed dispensaries, with licenses issued for both cultiva

AS-American_Samoa.summary: American Samoa does not have a legal medical or adult-use cannabis program. The territory maintains strict laws prohibiting the possession, cultivation, and use of cannabis, with criminal penalties still in place. Efforts to legalize or decriminalize cannabis have not gained significant legislative

AZ-Arizona.summary: Arizona has a dual cannabis program that includes both medical and adult-use marijuana, as well as a regulated hemp program. Medical cannabis was legalized in 2010 through the Arizona Medical Marijuana Act (AMMA), allowing qualified patients with a physician’s recommendation to obtain cannabis from

BC-British_Columbia.summary: British Columbia (B.C.), Canada, operates both a medical and adult-use cannabis program under federal oversight. Medical cannabis is regulated by Health Canada, allowing registered patients to purchase directly from federally licensed producers or grow their own with authorization. Adult-use cannabi

DC-District_of_Columbia.seProgramDetails: The Medical Cannabis Amendment Act of 2022 reserves 50% of all new medical cannabis business licenses (except Testing Laboratory licenses) for social equity applicants to support those impacted by cannabis criminalization in DC. To qualify, a business must have at least one DC resident owner with at least 50% ownership and meet two of three criteria: (1) be a returning citizen, (2) have a close family member with a cannabis/drug-related conviction, or (3) have an income below 150% of the median family income per HUD guidelines. Applications can only be submitted during open periods and must include a Social Equity Declaration Form, an Attestation Statement, and supporting documents. Approved applicants receive a 75% reduction in licensing fees for the first three years. The Alcoholic Beverage and Cannabis Administration (ABCA) determines eligibility based on the submitted proof.

DE-Delaware.seProgramDetails:
Reserved Licenses: A portion of available licenses are specifically designated for social equity applicants.
Reduced Fees: Applying for a marijuana business license can be expensive. This program reduces those fees for qualified applicants.
Community Investment: Tax revenue from marijuana sales will fund programs that support communities harmed by past marijuana laws. These programs might focus on:
Restorative justice initiatives
Reducing reliance on incarceration
Job training programs
Technology for expungement and civil rights restoration
Why It Matters
Fairness: This program levels the playing field by giving a chance to those who were impacted.
Community Repair: It supports communities disproportionately affected by past marijuana laws.
A More Diverse Industry: The marijuana industry isn't just for big corporations. This program fosters a more inclusive business landscape.

HI-Hawaii.seProgramDetails: The mission of the Social Equity Permitted Interaction Group is “To identify Hawaii communities and populations disproportionately impacted by cannabis criminalization and make recommendations for social equity and restorative justice policies that would help to reduce and remediate past and ongoing disparities, including equity in the market, community reinvestment, and expungement and resentencing.”

IL-Illinois.seProgramDetails: The Illinois Department of Commerce and Economic Opportunity (DCEO) established the Cannabis Social Equity Loan Program to provide financial assistance to social equity applicants. The program aims to support communities impacted by cannabis-related arrests through technical assistance and access to capital. Two rounds of Direct Forgivable Loans were launched, offering $8.75 million and $12 million to craft growers, transporters, infusers, and dispensaries. Loan amounts of up to $240,000 were available to qualified, licensed social equity dispensaries.
IL-Illinois.summary:
[Illinois Summary]
IL has two governing governing bodies. IDFPR handles dispensaries. DoA handles all other Cannabis licenses

LA-Louisiana.seProgramDetails: The state is currently in the process of passing legislation to ensure fair access to the legal cannabis industry for individuals and communities disproportionately affected by past cannabis enforcement.

MN-Minnesota.seProgramDetails: The Office of Cannabis Management (OCM) is reviewing license preapproval applications from verified social equity applicants, with the submission window closing on August 12 under Minnesota law. On October 16, OCM sent deficiency notices and requests for additional information to about 300 applicants, but no final determinations have been made yet. A "pending" status indicates ongoing review, and not receiving a deficiency notice does not imply approval or rejection. All applicants will receive a final notice of their qualification status after the full review, including compliance with true party of interest provisions. Verified social equity applicants could apply for preapproval, and qualified applicants will enter a lottery. The lottery will be scheduled once application reviews are complete, and selected applicants who pass final requirements, including a background check, will receive license preapproval. These preapproved applicants can then prepare for business operations pending rule adoption. OCM also plans to offer more social equity verification and licensing opportunities in the future, with details to be provided at a later date.

NB-New_Brunswick.summary: The Cannabis Management Corporation Act establishes the legal framework for the purchase, distribution, and sale of recreational cannabis in New Brunswick. Assented to on March 16, 2018, and coming into effect on June 25, 2018, the Act created the Cannabis Management Corporation (CMC), a Crown corpo

NJ-New_Jersey.seProgramDetails: The CREAMM Act stipulates that at least 15% of licenses across all license classes should be awarded to minorities, and 15% should be awarded to women and disabled veterans. Our commitment is to consistently surpass those benchmarks as the minimum for what will make New Jersey’s cannabis industry diverse, equitable, and inclusive.

RI-Rhode_Island.seProgramDetails:
The program aims to promote equitable participation and business ownership in the cannabis industry through financial assistance, reduced fees, and other forms of support.

The Social Equity Assistance Fund is created to provide grants, reduce or waive fees, and support initiatives like job training, mentoring, technical assistance, and restorative justice. The fund is financed by licensing fees, penalties, and interest, and it is administered by a commission in consultation with the cannabis advisory board. The commission will set eligibility criteria, qualifications, and processes for distributing funds.

Social equity applicants may qualify for up to 100% fee waivers, and those denied social equity status have opportunities to provide additional evidence or proceed as non-social equity applicants. Licenses granted to social equity applicants can only be transferred to other qualified social equity applicants, ensuring the program's benefits remain within its intended scope.

Annual reports to the governor and general assembly will track the program's effectiveness, including the number of grants awarded, financial assistance provided, new jobs created, and the economic impact. Licensed cannabis establishments must report annually on diversity in the industry and barriers to entry, with noncompliance subject to disciplinary action.

Overall, the program emphasizes reducing financial and systemic barriers to support equitable access to the cannabis industry while addressing the historical harms of cannabis law enforcement.

SD-South_Dakota.seProgramDetails: No existing Social Equity program currently in place.

TX-Texas.seProgramDetails: No existing Social Equity program currently in place.
TX-Texas.summary: There is one primary location where all regulated services—cultivation, manufacturing, and dispensing—are performed. Google labels "pickup" locations as retail, but these are actually meet-up points where patients collect pre-ordered products; on the website, they are referred to as "delivery" locat

UT-Utah.seProgramDetails: No existing Social Equity program currently in place.

VA-Virginia.seProgramDetails: The Virginia Cannabis Equity Business Loan Program and Fund was established to provide financial assistance to social equity qualified cannabis licensees, fostering business ownership and economic growth in communities disproportionately affected by cannabis prohibition. The Fund is a special nonreverting state treasury fund used exclusively to offer low-interest and zero-interest loans to these licensees, ensuring ongoing financial support without reverting funds to the general state budget. The Program, administered in partnership with a Community Development Financial Institution (CDFI), is responsible for identifying eligible businesses, distributing loans, providing technical assistance, and monitoring outcomes, with annual reports submitted to state officials on funding distribution and impact.

YT-Yukon.seProgramDetails: No existing Social Equity program currently in place.

global.description: Description
How to handle the transition from applicant to approved license, when license numbers are not assigned until approval. (e.g. New York)
If a source file is no longer being used, the licenses created by the source file but no longer updated by any other source files after a set period is inferred to be Inactive.

global.procedure: Deactivation: License No Longer In Source
global.description: If a license number stops appearing in any data source from a given locale, between one refresh and another (all else being equal), it is inferred to be inactive.
Raw data includes a field with license status information.

Determine the license status from the Expiration Date.
(Generally used when raw data contains Expiration Date, but not Status information)
Tan process when he receives file from USEARCH
Map location statuses provided by Locale to EI location statuses
The rule system dynamically determines and updates location status based on multiple factors including license status, entity type, business dates, internal notes, and more.

1. Open (Currently Operating)
A location is considered Open if:
It has an Active license and is not a pre-license entity,
OR
Today's date is after the listed open date, and either:
There is no close date, or
The open date is after the close date.
2. Comin"&"g Soon (Opening Soon)
If the location is not Open, mark it as Coming Soon if:
It has a Pending license and is not a pre-license entity.
3. Applying for License (In License Application Process)
If the location is not Open or Coming Soon, mark it as Applyin"&"g for License if:
It’s marked as a pre-license entity and has an Active license somewhere in its profile,
OR
It has a license with Applicant status.
4. Temporarily Closed
If none of the above statuses apply, set the location as Temporarily Closed if:
All "&"of its licenses are marked as Inactive
AND
All of its location statuses are marked Temporarily Closed.
5. Closed (Permanently Closed)
If none of the above apply, mark the location as Closed if:
All licenses are Inactive, and it’s not operating as a Hemp R"&"etailer or Testing/Research Lab,
OR
All licenses are Suspended,
OR
The current date is on or after the listed close date, and either:
There is no open date, or
The open date is before the close date.

1) If the location has a status in raw data: do not update.
2) If location only has all inactive licenses: location status = closed
3) If location only has only pending or applicant or pre-qualification licenses: location status = coming soon
